findings.title: Anyone can burn arbitrary users' tokens via the inverted allowance check in burnFrom

best_agent_match.title: Anyone can burn arbitrary users' tokens via the inverted allowance check in burnFrom

findings.title: Upgrade leaves the hidden legacy ledger live through non-deprecated write paths

best_agent_match.title: The upgrade switch leaves legacy balances and allowances spendable through bulk transfer helpers

rejected_candidates.title: Owner can steal all ERC20 tokens in the contract via acquire()
rejected_candidates.reason: `acquire()` only withdraws assets already held by the token contract itself; the contract has no deposit accounting, so this is a stray-asset recovery/admin-trust behavior rather than a vulnerability affecting tracked user balances.

rejected_candidates.title: Blacklisted users can still burn their tokens
rejected_candidates.reason: Allowing a blacklisted holder to destroy their own tokens does not let them transfer value or bypass the freeze in a way that creates realistic protocol harm.

rejected_candidates.title: Upgrade can irreversibly redirect all token operations to malicious contract
rejected_candidates.reason: Pointing upgrades at a malicious target is an owner-trust/centralization risk, not a permissionless bug; additionally, `upgrade()` is not irreversible because the owner can call it again and change `upgradedAddress`.

rejected_candidates.title: Bulk transfer functions bypass blacklist checks
rejected_candidates.reason: False positive: both bulk transfer variants eventually call `_transfer` and `_allowanceTransfer`, which enforce blacklist and pause checks.

rejected_candidates.title: Critical owner functions remain operational during pause
rejected_candidates.reason: The pause modifier is consistently applied to token-movement paths; administrative functions remaining callable is a design choice, not an exploitable protocol vulnerability by itself.

rejected_candidates.title: Immutable DOMAIN_SEPARATOR breaks permit on chain forks
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a speculative fork-compatibility concern rather than a realistic in-scope vulnerability causing present protocol harm.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing event emissions for role management
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a monitoring/transparency issue only and does not create direct protocol-level harm.

rejected_candidates.title: acquire() allows stealing ETH from contract
rejected_candidates.reason: Same root issue as the ERC20 `acquire()` candidate: it only recovers ETH held by the contract itself, with no user accounting or payable deposit flow to violate.
